headline: Chips Drag Wall Street Into the Red on the First Day of Q3
body_html:
Equities stumbled out of the gate in the third quarter as a brutal reversal in semiconductor shares overwhelmed pockets of strength elsewhere. The session was choppy from the open, and by the close the tape settled into a modest loss — a jarring contrast to the record-setting rally chip stocks enjoyed just weeks ago. Breadth was nearly dead-even across the 198-name universe, with 97 advancers facing 99 decliners, a split that underscored just how bifurcated the day's action truly was.

The sector rotation was stark. Software, Healthcare, and Consumer names each finished higher, collectively absorbing capital fleeing the Semiconductor group, which shed 5.49% — the worst reading across all eight sectors tracked. Homebuilders and Materials also struggled, down 3.58% and 3.10% respectively, as elevated input prices flagged by the latest factory-activity data continued to weigh on rate-sensitive and commodity-linked names.

Guardant Health stood apart as the session's signature mover, surging nearly 14% to lead all gainers. CrowdStrike followed with a double-digit advance of its own, reinforcing that software and security themes retained genuine buying interest even as the broader market wavered. As Q3 begins, the divergence between last quarter's darlings and today's laggards is the sharpest note investors will carry into tomorrow.

briefs.headline: Semiconductor Slide Wipes Out Q2 Momentum on Day One of Q3
briefs.body: After logging record gains through the second quarter, chip and chip-equipment names opened the new period with a sharp setback. KLA Corp and Teradyne each fell nearly 12%, while memory-sector names Sandisk and Micron dropped more than 10% apiece, dragging the broader Semiconductors group down 5.49% — the steepest sectoral loss of the session.

briefs.headline: Guardant Health Jumps Nearly 14%, Leads the Day's Gainers
briefs.body: The oncology-diagnostics company posted the largest single-session advance in the universe, climbing 13.82%. The move lifted the broader Healthcare sector to a 1.19% gain and drew attention to diagnostics as a relative bright spot at a time when other growth areas faced significant selling pressure.

briefs.headline: Corning Sinks More Than 13% as Materials Sector Buckles
briefs.body: Shares of the specialty glass and materials maker fell 13.62%, making it the session's steepest decliner. The drop compounded weakness across the Materials sector, which closed down 3.10%, as investors cited persistently elevated input prices noted in fresh US manufacturing data showing factory activity retreating from a four-year high.

briefs.headline: US–Iran Talks in Doha Put Strait of Hormuz in Focus
briefs.body: Diplomatic negotiations between Washington and Tehran concluded in Qatar, centering on access to the Strait of Hormuz — a chokepoint for a significant share of global oil flows. Energy markets monitored the outcome closely, though the Energy sector still closed down 0.97%, reflecting cautious positioning ahead of further clarity on any agreement.

briefs.headline: Gold Advances on Soft Labor Data and Fed Commentary
briefs.body: Bullion gained more than 2% after a weaker-than-expected jobs reading and remarks from Federal Reserve Chair Warsh prompted traders to reassess the rate outlook. The move offered a reminder that safe-haven demand remains latent beneath the equity surface, ready to re-emerge when macro signals soften.

briefs.headline: World Cup Effect May Have Padded June Payrolls by Tens of Thousands
briefs.body: Analysts at Goldman Sachs estimated that tournament-related activity could have temporarily inflated the June nonfarm payrolls figure by roughly 40,000 positions, complicating the signal investors draw from Friday's report. The caveat adds an unusual seasonal wrinkle to an already closely watched data release as the Fed weighs its next policy move.
